coredata.dc:title: Unleashing carbon quantum dots as novel nanocatalyst from waste plastic wrappers towards biodiesel production

coredata.dc:description: The accumulation of plastic waste poses a significant environmental threat, necessitating innovative strategies for its management and utilization. Carbon quantum dots derived from waste plastic wrappers were probed as novel nanocatalysts in the biodiesel synthesis using honge oil. The fluorescent solid catalyst was found to be highly active, with a higher yield of 99.52 % utilizing a mere 4 wt. % in the reaction. An extensive analysis of the catalyst was performed using different methods like XPS, BET, TGA, XRD, SEM-EDAX, FTIR, TEM, RAMAN, fluorescence microscopy, and spectroscopy. The fuel quality of the biodiesel produced, was observed to be within the commercial international standard. Kinetic investigations revealed pseudo-1st-order reaction kinetics, while thermodynamic analysis ascertained an endergonic, and nonspontaneous process. 1 kg of catalyst and 1 L of biodiesel cost $0.43 and $0.557, respectively. The E-metrics study confirmed the eco-friendly nature of the process. This unexplored area offers a sustainable solution that can help preserve nature and mitigate the challenges associated with conventional fuels. This research highlights the potential of recycling plastic waste into high-value catalysts, offering scope for its application in biodiesel synthesis from diverse non-edible oil feedstocks. Such advancements can contribute to a circular economy and address challenges linked to environmental pollution and sustainable fuel production.

head.ce:title: Unleashing carbon quantum dots as novel nanocatalyst from waste plastic wrappers towards biodiesel production

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Institute of Technology Durgapur, Durgapur 713209, West Bengal, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Institute of Technology Durgapur, Durgapur 713209, West Bengal, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accumulation of plastic waste poses a significant environmental threat, necessitating innovative strategies for its management and utilization. Carbon quantum dots derived from waste plastic wrappers were probed as novel nanocatalysts in the biodiesel synthesis using honge oil. The fluorescent solid catalyst was found to be highly active, with a higher yield of 99.52 % utilizing a mere 4 wt. % in the reaction. An extensive analysis of the catalyst was performed using different methods like XPS, BET, TGA, XRD, SEM-EDAX, FTIR, TEM, RAMAN, fluorescence microscopy, and spectroscopy. The fuel quality of the biodiesel produced, was observed to be within the commercial international standard. Kinetic investigations revealed pseudo-1st-order reaction kinetics, while thermodynamic analysis ascertained an endergonic, and nonspontaneous process. 1 kg of catalyst and 1 L of biodiesel cost $0.43 and $0.557, respectively. The E-metrics study confirmed the eco-friendly nature of the process. This unexplored area offers a sustainable solution that can help preserve nature and mitigate the challenges associated with conventional fuels. This research highlights the potential of recycling plastic waste into high-value catalysts, offering scope for its application in biodiesel synthesis from diverse non-edible oil feedstocks. Such advancements can contribute to a circular economy and address challenges linked to environmental pollution and sustainable fuel production.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic, a synthetic polymer, is one of the most important commodities worldwide and possesses numerous applications in different sectors. Its flexible, durable, and affordable nature makes it appear in a wide range of items, spanning from packaging and clothing to beauty products (Evode et al., 2021). However, the use of plastics is associated with numerous detrimental environmental impacts as a result of insufficient waste disposal and manufacturing methods. According to the United Nations report on the environment program, >430 million tonnes of plastic are produced by humanity annually, out of which two-third consist of short-lived products that soon turn into waste, contributing to ocean pollution and gradually entering the human food chain (Images, 2014). The primary domain where plastic has been extensively employed on a large scale is the packaging sector (Padgelwar et al., 2021). From the research conducted by the Business Research Company, it was found that the market size of global snack food wrapping is anticipated to propagate to $34.48 billion by the year 2027. The size was increased to $27.04 billion in 2023 from $25.45 billion in 2022. Future growth of the snack food packaging market will be driven by the increasing consumption of ready-to-eat food (Buisness Research Company 2023). By the year 2050, it is projected that Europe alone will produce 92 million tonnes of plastic packaging (Ncube et al., 2021). Current disposal methods for packaging plastics include landfilling, incineration, mechanical recycling, and chemical recycling (Oberoi et al., 2021; Davidson et al., 2021; Schyns and Shaver, 2021). However, these methods have significant limitations. Landfilling often leads to soil and water contamination, while incineration releases harmful greenhouse gases and toxic pollutants (Siddiqua et al., 2022). Mechanical recycling, which involves sorting and reprocessing plastics, is restricted by contamination and material degradation. Chemical recycling, although promising, remains costly and energy-intensive (Chen and Hu, 2024). As a result, only 9 % of plastic waste is recycled, leaving the majority to accumulate in the environment. Plastics with a five-year lifespan account for over two-thirds of waste, 40 % of which is derived from packaging (Rathor, 2023).

Hence, proper utilization of these can save nature and improve sustainability. One such method of proper utilization is the conversion of these wastes to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which are carbon-based nanoparticles having size of 1–10 nm, representing a new allotrope of carbon (Li et al., 2021). A multifaceted array of applications has made CQDs popular luminaries of the carbon spectrum over the last few years. In addition to their high efficiency at light harvesting and outstanding photo-induced electron transfer, CQDs exhibit several remarkable advantages, like stable chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity (Khan et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). These benefits render them a promising category of carbon nanomaterials with zero dimensions suitable for diverse applications, including solar cells, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, and biosensors (Mao et al., 2021). Additionally, the antibacterial properties of CQDs make them ideal candidates for use in antimicrobial coatings, water purification technologies, and medical treatments. Beyond these, CQDs can be utilized in various other fields, including gas sensing (Molaei, 2020), light-emitting diodes (Kim et al., 2023), phototherapy (Zhang et al., 2017), anti-counterfeiting measures (Yuan et al., 2024), lasers (Li et al., 2014), photocatalysis (Backes et al., 2025), drug delivery (Pourmadadi et al., 2024), gene transfer (Rezaei and Hashemi, 2021), agriculture (Peng et al., 2022), membrane separation (Nazari et al., 2024)etc. Recent advancements have also highlighted the integration of computational tools, such as machine learning, for optimizing the applications of CQDs. The remarkable versatility of CQDs has been pivotal in addressing critical challenges, including water remediation and electrochemical processes. Moreover, their significant role in electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution has garnered attention, showcasing their potential in sustainable energy production (El-Azazy et al., 2024). These advancements highlight the growing importance of CQDs in tackling global environmental and energy issues, paving the way for innovative applications in diverse fields. Over the past decade, numerous studies have explored the synthesis, characteristics, and diverse uses of CQDs reflecting the growing momentum in this area of research (Kang et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2022; Osman et al., 2023). Materials such as sugarcane bagasse (Parveen et al., 2024), fruit peels (Patel et al., 2024), and lignin (Myint et al., 2018), for example, have proven to be renewable and easy to process. But these antecedents don't deal with the increasing environmental impact of plastic garbage. Notably, no research has been undertaken so far on the synthesis of CQDs from waste plastic wrappers (WPW). WPW differ significantly from other precursors used for CQD synthesis, such as biomass, microorganisms, graphite, or chemicals. Unlike these conventional sources, WPW consist of layered, multi-polymer structures. This emphasizes the challenge and innovation of working with a mixture of polymers in the conversion process. This special makeup makes it possible to create CQDs with unique optical and structural characteristics, which could improve fluorescence and increase the range of practical uses. In addition, plastic wrappers are a serious environmental hazard since they are not biodegradable. Hence repurposing such materials addresses a critical waste management issue while contributing to high-value material production.

Since the industrial revolution, fossil fuel demand has skyrocketed, leading to both energy shortages and environmental pollution (Zoghi and Saidi, 2024). Biodiesel is extensively advocated as an affordable substitute to petroleum diesel fuel as a result of its numerous advantages, such as clean refineries, biodegradability, and its ability to reduce emissions caused by greenhouse gases and pollutants by up to 78 % (Masera and Hossain, 2023). Commercially, biodiesel is produced by the esterification/transesterification process, wherein various feedstocks undergo reaction with an alcohol in the occurrence of diverse catalysts, including homogeneous, heterogeneous, enzymatic, or nanocatalysts, resulting in fatty acid methyl ester (FAME; biodiesel) as a major product and H2O/glycerol as a by-product (Rhithuparna et al., 2024). Different catalysts of heterogeneous nature have been developed over the years to overcome the problems associated with conventional catalysts, facilitating both the easy dissociation of end products and the recovery of catalysts. They also have the advantage of being less corrosive and reusable (R. et al., 2024). Conversely, a unique class of heterogeneous catalysts called nanocatalysts can be employed in the production of biodiesel. They can enhance fuel quality and are used under benign operating circumstances, thereby decreasing energy usage. Additionally, the use of nanocatalysts increases reaction rates due to their high surface area and will resist saponification, contributing to long-term sustainability (Devasan et al., 2023). The use of a large number of nanocatalysts prepared from different sources has been reported so far in biodiesel manufacturing (Baskar et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022). However, WPW-derived CQDs as nanocatalysts in the synthesis of biodiesel is an unexplored area.

Batch experiments play a vital role in identifying the optimal parameters to maximize product yield. However, the traditional one-variable-at-a-time approach is labour-intensive and inefficient. To address these challenges, recent advancements have introduced innovative biodiesel optimization techniques, such as machine learning and computational chemistry. These cutting-edge methodologies enable predictive modelling and process optimization, significantly enhancing the efficiency and sustainability of biodiesel production (A.I. Osman et al., 2024). Additionally, computational tools like factorial designs have gained prominence for streamlining optimization. The Plackett-Burman design and Taguchi orthogonal array are effective in analysing the impact of individual factors on outcomes. Meanwhile, response surface methodologies (RSM), including Box-Behnken design (BBD) and central composite design (CCD), visualize interactions between variables through surface plots, offering a more comprehensive exploration of reaction conditions (Devasan et al., 2023). Despite its benefits, BBD has certain limitations, such as being non-rotatable and excluding extreme design points. CCD addresses these issues with its rotatable nature and inclusion of corner points, allowing for optimization beyond the initial design space and producing more accurate models. Finally, statistical validation using ANOVA ensures the reliability and accuracy of these models, further supporting the optimization process (Rhithuparna et al., 2024).

Consequently, WPW was employed as a precursor to make CQDs via thermal carbonization and sulfuric acid doping. This WPW derived sulfonated carbon quantum dots (WPW-CQD-SO3H) were used in the synthesis of honge oil methyl ester (HOME), commonly known as biodiesel from honge oil. Due to their nano-size effect and the presence of acidic groups, CQDs became suitable for use as an innovative heterogeneous catalyst to make biodiesel. The reaction was optimally allowed to occur by means of central composite design under response surface methodology (RSM-CCD), a computational technique. Under the optimized reaction condition, a high biodiesel yield was observed. Kinetic and thermodynamic investigations were performed to understand the order, endothermic and reaction spontaneity. The catalyst demonstrated impressive stability even after being reused four times, with minimal decline in its activity. The eco-friendly nature of the catalyst was studied using the E-metrics study. A low production cost of biodiesel and catalyst was estimated, suggesting that there are better chances for industrial-scale commercialization.

Waste plastic wrappers were obtained from the premises of the National Institute of Technology, Durgapur, India. Honge oil, procured from a native trader, was utilized for this study. Deionized water, potassium hydroxide (KOH), 2-propanol (purity ≥99.9 %), methanol (99.9 %), phenolphthalein, and sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98.07 %), were acquired from Merck, Mumbai, India Ltd. Chemicals and solvents of analytical quality sourced from commercial suppliers were employed without undergoing any purification steps.

CQDs were produced by thermal carbonization of WPWs, followed by doping with concentrated H2SO4, as shown in Fig. 1 . Initially, WPWs were washed, sundried, and cut into thin strips. It was then placed in an open ceramic crucible, which was heated up in a muffle furnace at 300 °C for 2 h to get the carbonized material. Subsequently, 4g of the carbonized WPW powder was immersed in 20 mL of H2SO4 and heated at 120 °C for 6 h with a stirring speed of 500 rpm to obtain a black solution containing CQDs. The solution underwent dilution with 300 mL of distilled water, then filtered using a Buchner funnel and a vacuum pump to isolate the residue. The CQDs underwent further purification by being washed in an ultracentrifuge with distilled water at an agitation speed of 10,000 rotations per minute (rpm) for 5 min until sulfate ions were undetectable. It was then oven-dried for 10 h at 60 °C, and the obtained sulfur-doped CQDs derived from waste plastic wrappers (WPW-CQD-SO3H) were used as a nanocatalyst for biodiesel production. Unlike porous carbons, which primarily serve as adsorbents or support structures for the catalyst production this CQD is made from waste plastic wrappers. The proposed method not only leverages the layered polymer structure of WPWs to create highly functionalized CQDs but also addresses the environmental challenge of plastic waste management, offering a sustainable and innovative solution.

The honge oil esterification reaction to its methyl ester using the WPW-CQD-SO3H catalyst was implemented in a 500-mL three-neck flat-bottom flask. One neck of the flask was joined to a thermometer, for monitoring the temperature while the other neck had a reflux condenser to ensure the recirculation of methanol back into the reaction mixture. Initially, a known volume of feedstock oil and methanol was added to the flask, which was heated until it reached the desired temperature. The agitation speed was kept constant (700 rpm) throughout the reaction. After reaching the required temperature, WPW-CQD-SO3H was included to the reaction mixture through the central neck and agitated for the designated time. Upon reaction completion, the catalyst was retrieved through filtration. The resulting mixture containing biodiesel, by-product water, and unreacted methanol/oil underwent washing with distilled water and rotary evaporation to separate biodiesel. The study involved varying the four parameters during the reaction: methanol weight % (wt.%), reaction time, catalyst wt.%, and reaction temperature. The optimum parametric conditions required to produce a high biodiesel yield were determined from a batch study performed, maintaining single parameter constant at a time. The yield of biodiesel was determined using Eq. (1) below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization methods of WPW-CQD-SO3H and HOME
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallinity of WPW-CQD-SO3H was confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement (2θ = 10–80°, step size = 0.05°), with a Cu Kα (λ = 1.541 Å) radiation source set at 40 kV and 300 mA in a D8 advance Bruker (Germany) instrument. The functional groups associated with WPW-CQD-SO3H were analysed via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra obtained with Spectrum 100 PerkinElmer (UK) apparatus. The surface characteristics and the elements present in them were probed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX), respectively, on a Prisma E, Thermo Scientific (USA) instrument. The CQD shape, size, and structure were further quantitatively analysed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) at a voltage of 200 KV with a Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA) instrument. The total surface area, pore size, and pore volume of WPW-CQD-SO3H were assessed using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, performed by the Micromeritics ASAP 2010 (USA) instrument. The optical properties of the WPW-CQD-SO3H catalysts were investigated by fluorescence spectrophotometry (FL) (SRS 3400, fluorescence spectrometer, Bruker, Germany) equipped with a 1 cm quartz cell at room temperature, acquisition of emission spectra in the interval 200–800 nm. To validate its thermal stability, WPW-CQD-SO3H underwent thermogravimetric (TGA) analysis using a Shimadzu instrument, model C30574700290 (Japan). The analysis was carried out between 30 °C −700 °C at 5 °C/min, under continuous nitrogen flow (19.8 mL/min). The fluorescent (FL) images of WPW-CQD-SO3H were recorded using Nikon Invitrogen fluorescence microscopy on an Eclipse 80i (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was executed on WPW-CQD-SO3H to identify the surface species attached to the catalyst. The analysis was carried out using ESCALAB Xi+ (USA) device of Thermo Fisher Scientific with Al Kα radiation. The structural information about the CQDs was further studied using Raman spectroscopy (excitation wavelength = 785 nm), which was recorded using a Horiba Jobin Yvon HR 800UV (Japan).

The synthesised HOME was examined using different analytical techniques like nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS), and FTIR. 1H and 13C NMR spectra were obtained at 28 °C using a 500 MHz JEOL ECZR series instrument. Tetramethyl silane was used as an internal standard for signal referencing. The GC–MS of HOME was conducted on an Agilent 6890 series GC system coupled with an HP 5791A mass-selective ionization interface from Santa Clara, USA. The column employed was an HP-5 ms capillary column, measuring 30 m in length with a diameter of 90.25 mm, and featuring a film thickness of 0.25 µm. The temperature of oven was set first at 150 °C for 2 min (heating rate of 4 °C/min), followed by an intensification to 300 °C, which was maintained steadily for 20 min. The injector and detector temperatures were 300 °C and 230 °C, correspondingly. The carrier gas used was helium, with a split ratio of 50:1 and a column flow rate of 1.5 mL/min. 1 µL of the sample was introduced into the system.

Following the batch study, the impact of different parameters, viz., methanol wt.%, reaction time, catalyst wt.%, and temperature, on the production of HOME was probed in accordance with the RSM-CCD approach. The experimental data is aptly modelled by means of a 2nd order approach through the application of CCD in RSM (Ao et al., 2024). CCD investigates the parametric effect on the resulting biodiesel yield and evaluates the model's accuracy (Rhithuparna et al., 2023). Five levels of each variable were examined, with values spanning from the lowest (-) to the highest (+), with the midpoint (0) as the focal point. For each variable, five levels (designated as -α, − 1, 0, +1, and +α) are tested, as detailed in Table 1 of the supplementary information file. Based on equation 2n + nc + 2n = 30, a total of 30 experiments were devised to analyse the correlations and mutual influences among these variables. Out of these 30 experiments, 6 experiments (nc = 6 runs) were repeated in the centre points, sixteen (2n = 16 runs) for the factorial points, and eight tests (2n = 8 runs) for the axial points.

To assess the effectiveness of each variable as well as to comprehend how their interaction influences biodiesel yield, intending to pinpoint the optimal response, a quadratic polynomial equation (Eq. (2)) was employed.

To optimize the factors used in HOME production and examine the efficacy of the regression model, a software-guided technique and regression analysis named statistical analysis of variance (ANOVA) were applied. In the meantime, the Fischer statistical test (F-value), P-value, and lack-of-fit data were also applied to investigate the efficiency and relevance of the created model. It was possible to examine the interactions between multiple independent factors for optimum yield from 3D surface response plots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the cost analysis of WPW-CQD-SO3H and HOME
ce:section.ce:para:
The cradle-to-grave cost analysis is a useful tool for tracking the expansion of the biodiesel industry. The cost and effectiveness of the catalysts have a big impact on the industrial-scale biodiesel production. For this reason, it's crucial to take into account the costs related to catalyst preparation to assess the method's viability. It considers the elements that have an impact on costs, like the method of manufacturing, raw material source, treatment procedure, and reusability. The total expense incurred in 1 kg of catalyst (ExC) production can be obtained from the generated equations below (Ghosh et al., 2024).

The XRD analysis was executed to ascertain the crystalline character of the WPW carbon and WPW-CQD-SO3H. WPW carbon (Fig. 2 a) exhibited more prominent crystalline peaks compared to the catalyst. A reduction in crystallinity of the catalyst suggests successful doping and functionalization, which are crucial for catalytic performance. The XRD pattern of both WPW carbon and WPW-CQD-SO3H showed the existence of significantly different peaks at 2θ = 27.7°, 36.2°, and 41.4°, indicating the distribution of carbon in the (002), (100), and (101) planes, respectively, on their surface. Additionally, the incorporation of sulfur in the WPW-CQD-SO3H matrix was confirmed by distinct peaks at 2θ = 15.5°, 32.5°, and 65.4°, which correspond to the (001), (110), and (112) planes of sulfur, respectively (Doroodmand et al., 2012). As shown in Fig. 2b, the WPW carbon and CQDs were analysed for surface area using N2 physio-sorption analysis at 77 K set up at a saturated vapour pressure of 750.58 mm Hg. The BET plot displayed a type IV isotherm pattern featuring a hysteresis loop. The catalyst exhibited 2.24 m2/g of surface area and the WPW carbon exhibited a very low surface area of 1.73 m2/g. According to the graph inset, for WPW-CQD-SO3H BJH (Barrett, Joyner, and Halenda) analysis determined the micropore volume to be 3.18×10−4 cm3/g and the pore size to be 6.86 nm. The respective outcomes have confirmed that WPW-CQD-SO3H is mesoporous in nature. The thermal stability of WPW carbon and WPW-CQD-SO3H was judged from thermogravimetric investigation (Fig. 2c). In both the cases, TGA showed a first-stage loss of mass (high volatility) occurring between of 50–150 °C due to the removal of moisture, adsorbed volatile components and plasticisers from the polymer (LPD Lab Services 2019). The predominant weight loss occurring between 150 and 500 °C in WPW-CQD-SO3H, characterized by low volatility, is attributed to the breakdown of -COOH, -SO3H, and -OH functional groups (Devasan et al., 2023). Whereas, the major weight loss from 150 to 450 °C in WPW carbon corresponds to the decomposition of organic matter and polymeric chains derived from the waste plastic wrappers. This suggests the breakdown of hydrocarbon structures in the carbon material. For WPW-CQD-SO3H, weight loss in the third step occurs due to the combustion of non-volatile matter such as carbon present in the catalyst. The remaining weight loss is due to the inorganic material, such as ash. In contrast, for WPW carbon, the weight loss slows down significantly beyond 450 °C temperature, suggesting the combustion of more stable carbonaceous materials. The fluorescence intensity was utilized to evaluate the optical characteristics of WPW-CQD-SO3H (Fig. 2d). The analysis was performed at an excitation wavelength of 200 nm. The catalyst emits light with the utmost intensity between 370 and 420 nm, exhibiting the best FL properties. The maximum peak is seen at 396 nm. The peak intensity depends on various parameters, such as the structure of the catalyst, synthesis parameters, etc. Fluorescence increased with respect to the substance concentration to a specific value and then decreased rapidly. Indications of more CQDs may arise from a higher FL intensity (Piasek et al., 2023). The functional groups present in WPW carbon and WPW-CQD-SO3H are determined from the FTIR spectrum, which is depicted in Fig. 2e. For WPW-CQD-SO3H, the stretching vibrations of SO3 − and OSO were noticed at 1018.4 cm−1 and 1114.9 cm−1, correspondingly, confirming that the CQDs contain –SO3H groups (Hu et al., 2021). The characteristic peaks of hydrothermal carbon such as CO (ester, ether, or hydroxyl), CH (aromatic), CC (alkenyl), and CO group (ester, quinone, carboxyl, or carbonyl) were observed at 1419.6 cm−1, 1516.6 cm−1, 1600.9 cm−1, and 1681.9 cm−1, correspondingly (Vi Tran et al., 2018). For WPW carbon, the peak at 721 cm−1 corresponds to single bond CH alkene stretches, while the peak at 1458 cm−1 indicates the presence of CC bonds. Additionally, the stretches at 1608 cm−1 and 1712 cm−1 represent CC bonds. The structural information about WPW-CQD-SO3H was further studied using Raman spectroscopy. The spectra (Fig. 2f) cover the "fingerprint" region affiliated with polyethylene and were obtained between 1000 and 1700 cm−1. Polyethylene features are present at 1054, 1093, 1183, 1298, 1395, 1438, 1480, and 1523 cm−1, respectively. The dominant bands are the CH twisting, and CC stretching vibration observed at 1298 cm−1, and 1093, 1183 cm−1, correspondingly (David et al., 1998). At 1395 and 1589 cm−1, the observed attributes characterize the D and G bands, correspondingly, indicating the presence of sp3 and sp2 carbons within CQDs (Doroodmand et al., 2012). The surface morphology of the carbon before doping and that of the carbon dots are shown in Fig. 3 .

As observed in Fig. 3a, the SEM images of carbon appear to have a smooth surface with uniform surface morphology. In Fig. 3b, 3c, the acid-doped carbon dots illustrate spherical morphology. The TEM images (Fig. 3d, e) confirmed this spherical nature with a uniform size distribution of the dots around 5–6 nm (determined using imageJ software). Carbon (87.2 %) and oxygen (12.8 %) are observed in the carbonaceous material before doping, as observed from the elemental mapping and EDAX analysis (Fig. 3f, g, h). Whereas, from the elemental mapping and EDAX data analysis of CQDs (Fig. 3i, j, k, and l), it was observed that they mainly consist of C (61.4 %), O (19.7 %), and sulfur (12.8 %) which confirms that CQDs are made up of a sulfonated oxygenous carbon structure. A fluorescence microscope fitted with a green fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) filter set was used to perform fluorescence imaging of the produced carbon dots (Fig. 4 a, b). Bright green fluorescent spots were visible in the obtained image, indicating that the sample comprised of carbon dots that were evenly dispersed. The application of image processing techniques, such as contrast enhancement and background subtraction, improved fluorescent spot quantification and visualization. The binding of the sulfonic acid group in WPW-CQD-SO3H was investigated by XPS analysis (Fig. 4c-f). C1 s, O1 s, and S2p peaks are visible in Fig. 4c of the broad XPS survey spectrum. Four peaks were noticed in the C1 s spectrum of the catalyst (Fig. 4d), which correspond to COO–, C – S, CO, and CC groups, at 289.48, 286.19, 285.37, and, 284.88 eV, respectively (Gouda et al., 2024). Three peaks were noticed in the O1 s spectra (Fig. 4e) that correspond to C – O, SO, and coordinated carboxylate CO groups at 534.18, 532.03, and 533.03 eV, respectively. The S2p spectra (Fig. 4f) showed peaks for –O–SO3H and –SO3H groups at 169.59 and 168.48 eV (Meng et al., 2020), respectively, as a result of spin orbit splitting in S2p as S2p1/2 and S2p3/2, confirming the existence of a sulfonic acid in WPW-CQD-SO3H.

Fig. 5 a and 5b illustrates the results of 1H and 13C NMR analyses conducted to ascertain the production and chemical structure of HOME. The peak occurrence at 3.55 ppm of the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 5a) validates the formation of methyl ester. This singlet peak represents the methoxy protons present in FAME. The presence of a peak (triplet) at 2.20 ppm is assigned to the α-CH2 protons present in FAME. As indicated by 13C NMR (Fig. 5b), the presence of peaks at 51.35, 174.22, and 127–130 ppm corresponds to the –OCH3 group, carboxyl peaks, and olefinic carbons substantiating the formation of FAME.

The FTIR spectrum of biodiesel synthesized using the WPW-CQD-SO3H catalyst is shown in Fig. 5c. The signals detected at 2924.08 and 2854.64 cm−1 correspond to the methylene band. Whereas, the distinct and prominent peak observed at 1739.79 cm−1 represents the carbonyl band, indicating the presence of ester groups in biodiesel. The peaks formed at 1462.04, 1373.31, and 721.37 cm−1 portray the aliphatic C–H bands, while the peak at 1165.00 cm−1 is attributed to the C–O stretching bands in FAME (Anjana et al., 2016).

A GC–MS analysis conducted on synthesized HOME (Fig. 5d) demonstrated its chemical composition. The components were also quantitatively assessed by comparing their peak areas with those of methyl heptadecanoate (C17:0, internal standard). The sharp and long peak observed at a retention time of 29.96 min constituted 16.32 % of the total area. The peak had an 89.39 % probability of being hexadecanoic acid, methyl ester, a component of FAME. Similarly, at a retention time of 29.23 min, there was a 42.78 % likelihood of observing 9,12-Octadecadienoic acid (Z, Z)-methyl ester. The possibility (84.66 %) of methyl stearate formation was observed at a retention time of 29.97 min. The remaining esters and their respective probability % found in FAME are detailed in Table 2 . The quality of the synthesised FAME was tested by different ASTM (American Society for Testing and Materials) characterization testing methods. According to Table 3 , the fuel characteristics are observed to meet the acceptable commercial standards for biodiesel as specified by ASTM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data analysis and experiment modelling using RSM
ce:section.ce:para:
An RSM matrix consisting of 30 experimental runs (Table S1- Supplementary Information File) based on the batch study was created in accordance with the software Design Expert to assess the impact of the four parameters used in this work on the production of HOME. According to the experimental runs, the actual yield % ranged from 78.96 to 99.52 %. The table also shows the software-predicted results for each experimental run. Biodiesel yield is influenced by factors l-O, as described in Eq. (23).

where ‘L’ represents the methanol wt. %, ‘M’ the time taken for the reaction, ‘N’ catalyst wt. %, and ‘O’ the reaction temperature. The "+" sign denotes a positive impact of the respective factor on the reaction, while the "-" sign highlights the negative influence of the factor in the reaction. As observed in Table 4 , catalyst loading is the highest contributing variable among the other three variables.

ANOVA is used to evaluate the significance of each variable and their interactions (Gouda et al., 2024). According to Table 5 , the selected model demonstrated statistical significance with a high F and low P value of 577.11 and 0.0001, respectively. Additionally, the P value can validate the interaction effects of chosen regression coefficients (Kolakoti et al., 2020), indicating the probability of error. The implication of the pertinent term in the model is indicated by a p-value below 0.05. It can be observed that all the quadratic terms, all the linear terms except methanol wt. %, and all the interactive terms except MeOH wt.% - catalyst loading were found to be significant. The preferred value of the coefficient of variation (C.V.) is less than 10 %, and for this model, it was found to be 0.388. This demonstrated a valid correlation amid the predicted and actual yield values. The model's precision value of 81.36 indicated a signal-to-noise ratio exceeding the recommended threshold of 4. This indicates that the model is appropriate for navigating within the design space. A high correlation exists amongst the selected model and the experimental data, as suggested by the coefficient of determination R2 (0.998).

The diagnostic plots (Fig. S1- Supplementary Information File) are utilized for evaluating the regression model's quality. Fig. S1a shows the plot of studentized residuals versus normal % probability of distribution. The yield responses exhibited a linear distribution consistent with a normal distribution, which corroborates the findings of the regression analysis. The predicted and actual values for each response were closely aligned as shown in Fig. S1b, indicating that the model was appropriate for the empirical data. A plot of residuals vs. experimental runs for 30 experiments is shown in Fig. S1c. There were no trends or patterns identified by independent residuals. The plot clearly showed the random distribution of the residuals around the center line and within the specific boundary. This indicated that the model is correct and precise, without any data errors. Studentized residuals are plotted against predicted yield in Fig. S1d. It was observed that the plot residuals were dispersed arbitrarily within the bounds of ± 4. This suggested that the model was deemed acceptable, and the predicted and actual values were independent of each other.

The potency of four independent variables on biodiesel manufacturing was studied using surface model plots (Fig. 6 ). Surface plots were analysed by observing two variables, keeping the other two constant at their center values. A hyperbolic relationship explains how the yield increased to reach its maximum with respect to the corresponding variables. Fig. 6a illustrates how biodiesel yield is affected from varying the reaction temperature and catalyst wt. %. The yield showed a notable increase when the temperature was raised to 160 °C (99.52 %). However, any further temperature rise led to a decrease in yield due to the evaporation of methanol (Ao et al., 2024). The impact of catalyst loading and MeOH concentration on biodiesel yield is shown in Fig. 6b. A linear correlation was observed between the factors up to their respective optimum values of 4 wt. % and 50 wt. %, beyond which it declined. Prolonging the reaction conditions past the optimum value decreased the yield because of the reversible character of esterification (Devasan et al., 2023). An overabundance of MeOH dilutes the system, impacting reactant interactions and conversion rates. In contrast, using small amounts of MeOH is insufficient to prevent the reaction from reversing. The relation between reaction time and catalyst wt. % is depicted in Fig. 6c. It was found that the yield increased with rise in time, and catalyst wt. % displaying a hyperbolic plane up to an optimum value of 3 h, and 4 wt. %, respectively, beyond which it reduced as a result of esterification reaction's reversible nature (Shobhana-Gnanaserkhar et al., 2020). Fig. 6d shows the correlation between reaction temperature and MeOH concentration. The yield increased with both parameters until it reached a peak of 99.52 % at 50 wt. % MeOH concentration and 160 °C, after which it began to slightly decrease. This decline occurred because the reaction mixture became overly diluted with excess methanol, which allowed water to enter and triggered the reverse reaction (Shu et al., 2010). The influence of reaction time and temperature on HOME yield is shown in Fig. 6e. A linear correlation is noted under the influence of temperature owing to the endergonic character of the esterification reaction with respect to reaction time. A maximum HOME yield was obtained when the temperature was increased to 160 °C and the time to 3h. The relationship between reaction time and MeOH wt. % is shown in Fig. 6f. At low methanol concentrations, increasing the reaction time from 2 to 4 h did not improve conversion efficiency. The highest HOME yield occurred at a 50 % MeOH concentration after 3 h, despite considerable variability, and adjusting the reaction time had minimal impact on the outcome.

A numerical optimization technique was adopted to determine the optimal input variables by selecting conditions satisfying greater levels of desirability (ranging from 0 to 1). By maintaining the variables within the upper and lower ranges, the optimization technique was intended to escalate the biodiesel yield. An optimal set of parametric conditions was generated by the RSM-CCD software for the conversion of honge oil to HOME. These conditions included 164.3 °C temperature, 48.15 wt. % MeOH concentration, 4.5 wt. % catalyst, and 3.5 h of duration, with a high yield of 99.6 %. Consequently, in the laboratory trial in triplicate, the regression model was applied with the optimum condition showing a yield of 99.2 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the kinetics and thermodynamics of HOME production
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a (plot between –ln(1-x) and time) reveals that biodiesel yield within the temperature range of 100 to 160 °C progresses linearly with respect to time (90 to 180 min) and adheres to the principles of pseudo-first-order kinetics. With higher temperatures, reactants are better able to contact one another, causing reaction rates to rise. The calculated k values adhere to Arrhenius' law, as indicated by the plot's high R2 value. The ln k vs. 1/T (Fig. 7b) plot reveals that the A and Ea. values are 79 min−1 and 32.02 kJ mol−1, respectively. The slope of Fig. 7c (ln k/T vs. 1/T plot) is used in Eq. (7) to determine the change in entropy and enthalpy of the reaction, which were, −0.2193 kJ K−1 mol−1 and 28.68 kJ mol−1, respectively. By using the obtained ΔS‡ and ΔH‡ values, the value of ΔG‡ was computed from Eq. (8) for all the temperatures as shown in Table 6 . The +ve and –ve values of ΔH‡ and ΔS‡, correspondingly, confirm that the reaction is endergonic and less disordered. The reaction was confirmed to be non-spontaneous based on the +ve value of ΔG‡, i.e., 123.67 kJ mol−1 at the ideal temperature (Hawash et al., 2019). The non-spontaneous nature of the reaction presents a handicap for its commercial application due to the energy required to make the process spontaneous. However, the amalgamation of natural energy sources like wind, solar, or tidal with the existing electrical energy technology could reduce the reaction's non-spontaneity by enhancing its efficiency and making the process more economical and sustainable in real-world applications (R. et al., 2024).

To investigate the recyclability of WPW-CQD-SO3H, it was separated and retrieved from the reaction mixture following each cycle. After filtration and methanol wash, the catalyst underwent oven drying at 60 °C for 5 h. Prior to being used in each cycle, the catalyst weight was checked. The next three consecutive catalytic cycles were then carried out using the same chemical reaction, which was maintained under optimal reaction conditions with the same recovery approach to assess reusability (Fig. 8 ). Each cycle demonstrated a decay in yield because of the leaching of the catalyst during washing and activation in the recycling process. Hence, a leaching test was performed by checking the catalyst weight before and after the reaction using Eq. (24). The observation indicated that, following the first and sixth cycles, 10 % and 48 % of the material leached, respectively. Consequently, in the 6th cycle of the reaction, the yield reduced from 99.52 to 69.73 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cost analysis for production of WPW-CQD-SO3H and HOME
ce:section.ce:para: The cost (in US dollars ($)) needed to produce 1-kilogram WPW-CQD-SO3H is determined using Eqs. (9)–(16). As observed in Table 7 , WPW-CQD-SO3H has six recycling opportunities. This characteristic can drastically lower the cost of preparation. Thus, the entire cost of producing 1 kg of catalyst for the current work, without reuse, comes to $2.58. Six reuses later, the price dropped to $0.43. Reusability is the only factor that can be held accountable for these significant cost savings. Table 7 also summarizes the step-by-step production cost of HOME. Using Eqs. (17)–(22), the total cost of production of 1 L HOME was observed to be $0.557. These findings underscore the economic benefits of developing and applying WPW-CQD-SO3H as a novel catalyst to make biodiesel from honge oil.

However, scaling up catalyst production to an industrial scale introduces several challenges. While sun-drying of plastic wrappers is practical in a laboratory setting, it becomes unfeasible for large-scale operations, particularly in regions with variable climates, requiring specialized drying equipment. Similarly, manual washing of raw materials or catalysts, which is manageable on a small scale, must be replaced with automated systems or industrial-scale washing facilities for larger production volumes. Additionally, logistical issues arise due to the bulk quantities of raw materials needed for industrial operations, which are not a concern in lab-scale work. Moreover, costs may vary significantly from region to region due to differences in local labour rates, energy costs, and infrastructure availability. For example, in regions with abundant solar energy, coupling solar energy with the current technology could reduce energy costs and improve overall efficiency. Similarly, areas with high tidal or wind energy potential could consider coupling these renewable sources with the process to reduce reliance on conventional energy, leading to cost savings. By utilizing the specific renewable energy resources available in different regions, it is possible to lower overall production costs and enhance the sustainability of the process. Furthermore, waste plastic wrappers, may be freely available locally for laboratory experiments but would require purchase for large-scale use, significantly increasing production costs. Size reduction, typically done manually in the lab, would necessitate equipment like ball mills and sorting screens at an industrial scale. The capital investment for such equipment could vary depending on the region's industrial development, with certain regions offering cost-effective solutions due to competitive manufacturing or favourable trade policies. These operational changes could substantially raise production costs, posing critical challenges that must be overcome to ensure scalability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: E-metrics studies on the reaction process
ce:section.ce:para:
The eco-friendly nature of the produced biodiesel employing the WPW-CQD-SO3H catalyst can be ascertained by studies based on green parameters like atom economy, environmental factor (E-factor), turn over frequency (TOF), and process mass intensity (PMI) (Devasan et al., 2023; Kondawar and Rode, 2017; Maurya and Sharma, 2024; Arora et al., 2021).

The mass ratio of waste generated to product acquired is known as the e-factor. An approach with a lower E-factor value is considered eco-friendly. The mass of HOME produced relative to the reactants utilized, on the other hand, is known as atom economy. Alternatively, the PMI value is determined by dividing the mass of HOME generated by the total amount of reactants consumed in the esterification process. Lower PMI and E-factor values signify less waste generation during the procedure. TOF is the catalyst's instantaneous efficiency. TOF value shows the ability of the catalyst to convert the reacting substances into products at a rapid rate. Recyclability of the catalyst is not taken into account while computing the green parameters. The estimated E-factor, PMI, TOF, and atom economy are 0.66, 1.66, 0.009 h−1 and 61.71 %, respectively, demonstrating the process's eco-friendliness and reduced waste production (Calculations are shown in Supplementary information file). In terms of energy consumption, the overall process is designed to be as energy-efficient as possible, ensuring a favourable balance between sustainability and performance. In the future, if alternative methodologies, such as ultrasonic-assisted reactions, are explored (A.I. Osman et al., 2024), energy consumption could be further reduced, potentially enhancing the overall eco-friendliness of the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative study of WPW-CQD-SO3H with other reported works
ce:section.ce:para: The literature has shown that different varieties of heterogeneous catalysts can be employed for the eco-friendly manufacturing of biodiesel. An overview of the pertinent information, including the reaction conditions, feedstocks, catalyst type, and biodiesel yield, is summarised in Table 8 to compare it with WPW-CQD-SO3H. Most of these factors evidenced fruitful in transforming the feedstocks into biodiesel. However, the limitations include long reaction time (entry 5), complex catalyst production process (entries 1 to 5) and lower yield in comparison to the developed catalyst. It is imperative to emphasize the unique eco-friendly aspect of this approach. Unlike conventional carbon dot catalysts synthesized from chemical precursors, WPW-CQD-SO3H is derived from waste plastic wrappers. When benchmarked against biodiesel production using Prunus Armeniaca seed oil with the SrO-La2O3 catalyst (Entry 6) and Carthamus lanatus L. seed oil with the CoWO3@rGO catalyst (Entry 7), which achieved a similar yield of 97.28 % and 99.7 % respectively, the WPW-CQD-SO3H catalyst demonstrates a significant advantage in terms of cost-effectiveness and sustainability. The production of the SrO-La2O3 and CoWO3@rGO catalyst relies on expensive and less readily available chemicals such as lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate, strontium nitrate, cobalt and tungsten based compounds respectively. These high-cost precursors not only increase the overall production cost but also limit the economic feasibility of scaling up such processes. In contrast, the WPW-CQD-SO3H catalyst uses inexpensive and widely available sulphuric acid, along with discarded waste plastic wrappers as the primary raw material, making it a far more cost-effective and sustainable alternative. This low-cost approach reduces the financial burden associated with catalyst synthesis while simultaneously addressing the critical issue of plastic waste management. Entries 8, 9, and 10 showed better biodiesel yields, but still less than the present work. However, prolonged exposure to cadmium, bismuth, and molybdenum compounds can cause health problems. Cadmium is highly toxic, especially when inhaled as dust or fumes, and poses significant environmental and health risks. The production of MoO3 nano catalysts involves high-temperature calcination processes, which are energy-intensive and less sustainable. Molybdenum-based catalysts may also require strict handling protocols due to their potential environmental toxicity and bioaccumulation risks. In contrast, the work reported by Munir et al. (Entry 11) demonstrates high biodiesel yield of 97.6 % under relatively mild parametric conditions when compared to the present work. However, the Al2O3 nanocatalyst is not particularly novel in biodiesel research. Furthermore, the study does not provide critical details such as the surface area of the catalyst, which restricts a comprehensive evaluation of its performance and efficiency metrics. Extensive works are reported on honge oil using different heterogeneous catalyst such as entries 12–16 due to their abundant availability and cost effective option. However, challenges such as its high free fatty acid content and the need for eco-friendly catalytic systems remain unaddressed. This study overcomes these challenges by employing an innovative sulfur-doped CQD catalyst derived from waste plastic wrappers. This innovative utilization of waste materials provides a sustainable catalyst production method. Moreover, the utilization of waste plastic, found accumulating in landfills, rivers, ponds and oceans, for catalyst synthesis, heralds a sustainable solution in preserving nature's integrity. Thus, the present work represents a significant advancement towards environmentally responsible catalysis in biodiesel production.

A bibliographic study is a method of research that provides a comprehensive summary and categorization of past and present work related to a specific topic, enabling a more profound comprehension and prediction of future research fields. The bibliometric analyses presented in this study were grounded on a wide-ranging, multidisciplinary compilation of bibliographic data from cutting-edge scientific papers contained in the online database Science Citation Index, i.e., Scopus. This study was performed by conducting three sets of searches spanning the period from 2014 to 2024 from the SCOPUS collection data repository. Only research articles published in English were considered for the study. The study was evaluated using the software VOSviewer. Set 1 is the search carried out by keeping the keywords ‘biodiesel and nanocatalyst’. The major aim was to identify the advanced stages of biodiesel synthesis, examine the use of nanocatalysts, and acquire insightful knowledge on the trajectory of research progress, upcoming obstacles, and current interests in this domain. The most predominant research topics and emerging trends were probed in order to analyse the articles that were gathered, taking into account the keywords, authors' list, co-occurrence, publication number, number of citations/nation, etc. A total of 400 papers were analysed in set 1. The minimum number of occurrences of the keyword count in an article was kept at 10. The prominent pink cluster (Fig. 9 a) dominated by ‘biodiesel’ highlights the interconnection between nanocatalysts, affirming its status as an emerging area of research. Key words, including heterogeneous catalysts, reusability, fatty acid methyl ester, and cost effectiveness, are also encompassed in this cluster. The second green cluster highlights biofuel production and nanotechnology, presenting another emerging research theme in this domain. Set 2 is a search conducted by using the keywords ‘biodiesel and carbon dots’, resulting in a total of 21 articles. The minimum number of occurrences of the keyword count in an article was kept at 5. This reduced the number of keywords with higher link strengths to 8, which were clustered into 2 groups. The blue cluster (Fig. S2a), dominated by ‘biodiesel’, demonstrates an interconnection with the pink cluster, which represents the carbon dot. This knowledge map clearly shows that a limited number of studies have explored the connection between carbon dots and biodiesel, confirming it's an emerging topic. Fig. S2b indicates the countries in which the papers were published based on the number of publications and citations of the selected papers. In Set 3, is the search was conducted using the keywords ‘biodiesel and plastic’. The red cluster (Fig. 9b), primarily centered around "biodiesel," represents the most critical aspect of this research area. Terms like waste plastic, polyethylene, polypropylene, plastic recycling, etc. are interconnected with each other in this cluster, indicating a significant relationship between biodiesel and plastics, which represents a promising trend. The second-largest green cluster is dedicated solely to plastics, highlighting the emerging research area of waste management and recycling. The blue cluster includes keywords such as feedstocks, catalysts, fatty acid methyl ester, etc. The pink cluster focuses on reinforced plastics, esterification, biodiesel industry, etc.

In this study, waste plastic wrappers were converted into sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots. These CQDs were used as nanocatalysts for biodiesel manufacturing by means of the esterification of honge oil. The crystalline mesoporous catalyst had a surface area of 2.24 m2/g. The presence of –SO3H groups in the catalyst was confirmed by FTIR and XPS analysis. The high florescence intensity indicated the formation of more CQDs. Raman spectroscopy showed D and G bands in WPW-CQD-SO3H, indicating the presence of sp3 and sp2 carbons within CQDs. The catalyst was found to be highly active, with a higher yield of 99.52 % utilizing 4 wt. % catalyst and 50 wt. % MeOH at 160 °C for 3 h. The fuel quality of HOME was found to be within the commercial international standard. Pseudo-1st-order kinetics were observed with an activation energy of 32.02 kJ mol−1. The catalyst was observed to be cost effective and reusable without much decrease in its activity. The bibliometric analyses conducted helped to enable a more profound comprehension and prediction of future research fields. The eco-friendliness of the procedure was validated by e-metrics research. The CQDs developed in this study could also find applications in areas such as drug delivery, photocatalysis, bioimaging, water purification etc. which needs to be explored. These additional applications could enhance the utility and commercial value of the process beyond biodiesel production. Despite the promising results, several challenges remain in the application of WPW-CQD-SO3H for biodiesel synthesis. One of the primary hurdles is the scalability and practical implementation of this process as discussed in Section 6. Another significant challenge lies in the sourcing and pre-treatment of waste plastic, as the variability in plastic composition could affect the uniformity and quality of the catalyst. Moreover, although the process is confirmed to be eco-friendly through the E-metrics study, the energy required for catalyst preparation and the reaction itself could impact the overall environmental benefits, especially if not optimized for energy efficiency. The endergonic nature of the process also highlights the need for additional energy input, which must be carefully balanced to ensure sustainable operation. Future studies should concentrate on investigating different kinds of waste plastics and feedstocks to expand the applicability of the process. Examining hybrid catalytic systems or alternate reaction routes may increase productivity and range of products. Furthermore, a thorough techno-economic analysis and life-cycle assessment of the process will help verify its environmental effect and scalability. This novel approach exemplifies the potential of waste recycling to mitigate environmental pollution while advancing renewable energy technologies. The future of waste recycling lies in integrating such technologies into broader green energy initiatives. By scaling up the conversion of waste plastics into carbon quantum dot catalysts, this process can contribute significantly to global energy sustainability. Expanding the scope of this technology to other non-edible oil feedstocks can further enhance its impact. A focus on green energy solutions derived from waste materials aligns with the circular economy principles and fosters sustainable development. To facilitate the adoption of this technology, it is essential to integrate it into existing waste management and renewable energy policies. Policy frameworks should incentivize the conversion of waste plastics into valuable products like biodiesel, thereby promoting waste reduction and renewable energy generation. Implementing supportive regulations and providing financial incentives can encourage industries to adopt such sustainable practices. Additionally, public awareness campaigns can educate communities about the benefits of waste-to-energy technologies, fostering broader acceptance and participation.
D. Rhithuparna: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Gopinath Halder: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
